paymentPlan.title: Payment Plan
paymentPlan.noPlan: No payment plan exists. Create one.

installments.title: Installments
installments.confirmDelete: Are you sure you want to delete this installment?
installments.addSuccess: Installment added — existing amounts adjusted proportionally
installments.amountExceedsMax: Amount exceeds the available balance. Existing installments must keep a minimum amount.
installments.notesOnlyWarning: In an active plan you can only edit notes

templates.title: Payment Templates
templates.offPlanCondensedDesc: Reservation → Down Payment → Frame → Completion → Final
templates.lumpSumDesc: Full amount in a single payment

loanTracking.title: Bank Loans
loanTracking.addLoanDesc: Record a new bank loan for this payment plan.

actions.terminal: This cheque is in a terminal state.

tabs.cashFlowTooltip: Per-installment breakdown — see how each payment loses value over time
tabs.scenariosTooltip: Compare 4 payment methods (cash, off-plan, loan, current) side by side
tabs.pricingTooltip: Price recommendation that compensates for the time cost of money
tabs.whatIfTooltip: Drag sliders to test different down payment and duration combinations
tabs.sensitivityTooltip: Tornado chart and heat map — see which variables affect NPV the most
tabs.dscrTooltip: Check the buyer's ability to repay the loan even when interest rates rise
tabs.drawScheduleTooltip: Model staged construction loan disbursements and interest reserve depletion
tabs.monteCarloTooltip: Run thousands of random scenarios to see the full range of possible outcomes
tabs.forwardCurveTooltip: Market expectations for future interest rates based on ECB data
tabs.hedgingTooltip: Compare interest rate protection strategies: floating, swap, cap, collar
tabs.settingsTooltip: Configure discount rates, Euribor sources, and bank spread

cashFlow.label: Description
cashFlow.dfTooltip: Discount Factor — shows how much 1€ paid in the future is worth today
cashFlow.pvTooltip: Present Value — the real value of each installment in today's money
cashFlow.infoBanner: This table shows how each installment loses value over time. Money today is worth more than the same money in the future — because you could invest it in the meantime.
cashFlow.rowTooltip: The installment "{label}" is paid in {days} days. Due to the wait, its real value is {pv} instead of {amount} — a loss of {loss}.
cashFlow.rowTooltipToday: The installment "{label}" is paid today, so it loses no value.
cashFlow.summaryLoss: Total value loss: {loss} ({percent}) — this money is "lost" because installments are paid gradually instead of upfront.
cashFlow.summaryNoLoss: No value loss — all payments are made immediately.
cashFlow.npvTooltip: Net Present Value — the real total you receive in today's money
cashFlow.lossTooltip: The difference between face value and present value — how much you "lose" on each installment
cashFlow.nominalTotalTooltip: The original total amount without factoring in the time value of money

scenarios.npvTooltip: Net Present Value — how much money you actually receive in today's value
scenarios.costTooltip: Time Cost — how much money you lose because you are not paid immediately
scenarios.wacpTooltip: Weighted Average Collection Period — how many days on average you wait to collect your money
scenarios.infoBanner: Compare 4 payment methods. Each scenario shows how much you actually receive and how much is "lost" due to waiting. The green scenario is the best for you.
scenarios.rowTooltipCash: Cash: The buyer pays everything today. You lose nothing — this is the ideal case.
scenarios.rowTooltipOffPlan: Off-Plan: The buyer pays gradually during construction. You lose {cost} due to waiting.
scenarios.rowTooltipLoan: Loan: The buyer pays 30% own funds + 70% via bank. You lose {cost} due to waiting.
scenarios.rowTooltipCurrent: Current Plan: The installment plan you have agreed on. You lose {cost} due to waiting.
scenarios.bankComparisonText: If you borrowed {amount} for {days} days at {rate} interest, you would pay {interest} in interest. This is the real cost of waiting in banking terms.

pricing.nominalPriceTooltip: The original sale price without any adjustment
pricing.timeCostTooltip: The money you "lose" because the buyer doesn't pay everything today. Think of it as the interest you would earn if you had the money in your bank account.
pricing.wacpTooltip: How many days on average you wait until you collect the money. The higher the number, the greater the loss.
pricing.effectiveRateTooltip: The annual percentage of value you lose due to waiting. If this is higher than your bank interest rate, you are losing money.
pricing.infoBanner: This price compensates for the value loss from gradual payments. If you sell at this price, you will actually receive what the property is worth — no more, no less.
pricing.whatThisMeansText: If you sell at the nominal price ({nominal}) with installments, you actually receive only {npv}. To truly get {nominal}, you need to sell at {recommended}.
pricing.disclaimer: Pricing recommendation based on Net Present Value (NPV) of future cash flows. This is not financial advice.

whatIf.infoBanner: Try different payment scenarios. Drag the sliders to see how value loss changes depending on the down payment percentage and repayment duration.
whatIf.upfrontTooltip: The percentage the buyer pays immediately — a higher down payment means less value loss for you
whatIf.monthsTooltip: How many months the buyer takes to pay the remaining amount — shorter is better for you
whatIf.resultNpvTooltip: The real value of money you will receive, adjusted for time — like depositing everything in a bank today
whatIf.resultLossTooltip: How much value you lose because the buyer pays gradually instead of all at once
whatIf.resultRecommendedTooltip: The sale price that compensates for the time cost — sell at this price to break even
whatIf.resultWacpTooltip: The average number of days you wait to collect payment — shorter means less risk

alert.highLoss: Value loss exceeds {threshold}! Consider requesting a higher down payment or increasing the sale price.

settings.euriborRatesTooltip: ECB reference rates used to calculate the time cost of money. Updated daily from European Central Bank data.
settings.discountSourceTooltip: Choose which interest rate to use as the baseline for calculating present values. Most common: Euribor 3M + bank spread.
settings.bankSpreadTooltip: The extra percentage banks add on top of Euribor when lending. Greek banks typically charge 2-4% spread. Affects your effective discount rate.
settings.manualRateTooltip: Enter your own discount rate manually if you prefer a specific rate instead of automatic ECB data.

sensitivity.infoBanner: See which variables have the greatest impact on your NPV. The tornado chart ranks variables by influence; the heat map shows how two variables interact.
sensitivity.tornadoTitle: Tornado Chart — Variable Impact on NPV
sensitivity.tornadoTitleTooltip: Each bar shows how much the NPV changes when a single variable swings ±20%. Longer bars = more sensitive.
sensitivity.heatMapTitleTooltip: Pick two variables for the axes. Each cell shows the NPV for that combination — red is low, green is high.
sensitivity.rowVariableTooltip: The variable shown on the rows of the heat map table
sensitivity.colVariableTooltip: The variable shown on the columns of the heat map table
sensitivity.varDiscountRateTooltip: The interest rate used to convert future money into today's value. Higher rate = more loss.
sensitivity.varBankSpreadTooltip: The extra % banks add on top of Euribor. Typical range in Greece: 2-4%.
sensitivity.varSalePriceTooltip: The property's sale price. Higher price = larger absolute NPV.
sensitivity.varUpfrontPercentTooltip: Percentage paid immediately. More upfront = less value loss over time.
sensitivity.varPaymentMonthsTooltip: How many months until full payment. Longer duration = more value loss.
sensitivity.varCertaintyMixTooltip: Confidence weighting for uncertain future payments. 1.0 = fully certain, lower = risky.

dscr.infoBanner: Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR) measures the buyer's ability to service debt. Enter the loan details to see how rate increases affect debt coverage.
dscr.title: DSCR Calculator
dscr.noiTooltip: Annual net operating income — rental income or buyer's annual income available for debt service
dscr.loanAmountTooltip: The total amount borrowed. Default is 70% of the sale price (typical bank financing).
dscr.annualRateTooltip: The annual loan interest rate. Used to calculate monthly payments and stress test results.
dscr.loanTermYearsTooltip: How many years to repay the loan. Typical Greek mortgage: 20-30 years.
dscr.annualDebtServiceTooltip: Total loan payments per year (principal + interest). The buyer needs income at least this much to stay safe.
dscr.monthlyPaymentTooltip: The fixed monthly payment (principal + interest) based on current rate and term.
dscr.shockBpsTooltip: Basis points added to the base rate. 100 bps = 1%. Shows how rate increases stress the loan.
dscr.stressedRateTooltip: The new interest rate after adding the shock amount to the base rate.
dscr.dscrLabelTooltip: Debt Service Coverage Ratio — income divided by debt payments. Below 1.0 means the buyer can't cover their loan.
dscr.gaugeZones: Gauge zones: Red (<1.0) = Danger, Orange (1.0–1.2) = Warning, Blue (1.2–1.4) = Adequate, Green (>1.4) = Safe

drawSchedule.infoBanner: Model the staged disbursement of a construction loan. Enter loan terms and draw events to see interest accrual, reserve depletion, and total cost of capital.
drawSchedule.totalCommitmentTooltip: The maximum amount the bank has agreed to lend for the project. Not all of it is drawn at once.
drawSchedule.annualRateTooltip: The annual interest rate on the construction loan. Interest is charged only on the amount drawn.
drawSchedule.interestReserveTooltip: Money set aside to pay interest during construction. When this runs out, you must pay interest from other funds.
drawSchedule.closingDateTooltip: The date the loan agreement is signed and the first draw can be requested.
drawSchedule.maturityDateTooltip: The date by which the full loan must be repaid or refinanced. Usually 18-36 months for construction.
drawSchedule.originationFeeTooltip: A one-time fee the bank charges for setting up the loan, calculated as a percentage of total commitment.
drawSchedule.interestAccrualTooltip: Simple: interest is calculated only on the principal. Compound: interest on interest (more expensive).
drawSchedule.label: Label
drawSchedule.emptyState: No draw events. Click "Template" to load a typical Greek construction schedule, or add entries manually.
drawSchedule.reserveWarning: Interest reserve is exhausted at month {month}. Additional cash needed: {shortfall}.
drawSchedule.totalDrawnTooltip: Sum of all draw amounts — the total money actually disbursed from the loan.
drawSchedule.totalInterestTooltip: All interest paid during the construction period. Depends on how much is drawn and for how long.
drawSchedule.originationFeeLabelTooltip: The one-time bank setup fee. Paid at closing, adds to total cost of capital.
drawSchedule.totalCostOfCapitalTooltip: Interest + origination fee — the total price you pay for borrowing this money.
drawSchedule.costPercentTooltip: Total cost as a percentage of total drawn. Lets you compare with other financing options.
drawSchedule.waobTooltip: Weighted Average Outstanding Balance — the average loan balance over time, accounting for staged draws.

monteCarlo.infoBanner: Run thousands of random scenarios to understand the range of possible NPV outcomes. Configure which variables to perturb and their probability distributions.
monteCarlo.scenariosTooltip: Number of random scenarios to generate. More = more accurate results but slower. 10,000 is a good default.
monteCarlo.seedTooltip: A fixed number that makes the random results reproducible. Same seed = same results every time.
monteCarlo.meanNpvTooltip: The average NPV across all simulated scenarios. Think of it as the "most likely" outcome.
monteCarlo.stdDevNpvTooltip: Standard Deviation — measures how spread out the results are. Higher = more uncertainty.
monteCarlo.p10Tooltip: 10th percentile — 90% of scenarios are better than this. Your worst realistic case.
monteCarlo.p50Tooltip: 50th percentile (median) — half the scenarios are above, half below. The middle outcome.
monteCarlo.p90Tooltip: 90th percentile — only 10% of scenarios are better. Your best realistic case.
monteCarlo.minMaxTooltip: The absolute minimum and maximum NPV from all scenarios — shows the full spread.
monteCarlo.probPositiveTooltip: Percentage of scenarios where you receive more than zero present value. Should be close to 100%.
monteCarlo.executionTimeTooltip: How long the simulation took to run. Depends on scenario count and your device.
monteCarlo.fanChartLegend: Bands show P10–P90 confidence intervals: green = optimistic, blue = median, red = pessimistic. The wider the fan, the more uncertain the outcome.
monteCarlo.histogramLegend: Bars show how often each NPV range occurred. The orange line is the cumulative probability (CDF) — shows what % of scenarios fall below each value.

forwardCurve.error: Failed to load forward curve data.
forwardCurve.infoBanner: The forward curve shows market expectations for future interest rates. Spot rates are current ECB Euribor rates; forward rates are derived using the no-arbitrage formula.
forwardCurve.chartTitleTooltip: This chart shows current and expected future interest rates. Useful for deciding when to lock in rates.
forwardCurve.chartTitle: Yield Curve — Spot vs Forward Rates
forwardCurve.tenorTooltip: The time period of the rate (e.g. 1M = 1 month, 12M = 1 year). Longer tenors usually have higher rates.
forwardCurve.spotLineTooltip: Current ECB Euribor rate for this tenor — what the rate IS today.
forwardCurve.forwardLineTooltip: Expected future rate derived from the yield curve — what the market THINKS the rate will be.

shape.normalTooltip: Rates increase with longer tenors — the normal state. Investors demand more for longer commitments.
shape.invertedTooltip: Short-term rates are higher than long-term — often signals an expected economic slowdown.
shape.flatTooltip: Rates are similar across all tenors — the market is uncertain about direction.
shape.humpedTooltip: Medium-term rates are highest — unusual shape suggesting mixed expectations.

hedging.infoBanner: Compare 4 interest rate hedging strategies: unhedged floating, fixed-rate swap, cap, and collar. Adjust parameters and rate scenarios to find the optimal protection strategy.
hedging.notionalTooltip: The loan principal amount on which interest is calculated. Default is 70% of sale price.
hedging.termYearsTooltip: How many years the hedging contract covers. Match this to your loan term.
hedging.swapRateTooltip: The fixed rate you would pay in an interest rate swap. You pay this fixed rate instead of the floating Euribor.
hedging.scenarioTooltip: A projection of how interest rates will change over the term. Rising = rates go up each year.
hedging.capStrikeTooltip: The maximum rate you'll pay with a cap. If rates rise above this, the bank covers the difference.
hedging.capPremiumTooltip: The annual cost of the cap protection — like an insurance premium you pay regardless.
hedging.collarRangeTooltip: Floor = minimum rate you'll pay (even if rates drop). Cap = maximum rate. The narrower the range, the cheaper the premium.
hedging.collarPremiumTooltip: Annual cost of the collar. Usually cheaper than a cap because you give up gains below the floor.
hedging.strategyTooltip: The hedging approach. Each has different risk/cost trade-offs.
hedging.strategyFloatingTooltip: No protection — you pay whatever the market rate is. Cheapest when rates stay low, most expensive when they rise.
hedging.strategySwapTooltip: Exchange your floating rate for a fixed rate. Total certainty on costs, but you miss out if rates drop.
hedging.strategyCapTooltip: Sets a ceiling on your rate. You pay a premium, but if rates spike above the cap, you're protected.
hedging.strategyCollarTooltip: Sets both a floor and ceiling. Cheaper than a cap, but you won't benefit from rates dropping below the floor.
hedging.totalCostTooltip: The total interest paid over the full term under this strategy, given the selected rate scenario.
hedging.avgAnnualTooltip: Average annual interest cost — total cost divided by term years. Useful for budgeting.
hedging.avgRateTooltip: The effective average interest rate you end up paying over the full term.
hedging.breakEvenTooltip: The rate at which the swap and floating cost the same. If you expect rates above this, swap is better.
hedging.breakEvenText: At a constant floating rate of {rate}%, the swap and floating strategies cost the same.

waterfall.title: Equity Waterfall
waterfall.infoBanner: Model the distribution of project profits between LP (Limited Partner) and GP (General Partner) through tiered waterfall structures.
waterfall.lpEquityTooltip: Limited Partner equity — the money invested by the passive investor (e.g. you, the property owner).
waterfall.gpEquityTooltip: General Partner equity — the money invested by the active manager/developer.
waterfall.totalProceedsTooltip: Total money received when the project is sold or completed. This is what gets distributed.
waterfall.projectYearsTooltip: How long the project takes from investment to exit. Used to calculate IRR (annualized return).
waterfall.lpFirstReturnTooltip: If checked, the LP gets their initial investment back before any profits are split. Standard in real estate.
waterfall.hurdleRateTooltip: The return threshold for this tier. Profits below this rate go to the previous tier's split.
waterfall.lpShareTooltip: LP's share of profits in this tier (0-1). E.g., 0.80 means LP gets 80% of this tier's profits.
waterfall.gpShareTooltip: GP's share of profits in this tier (0-1). E.g., 0.20 means GP gets 20% of this tier's profits.
waterfall.lpTotalTooltip: Total money the LP receives from all tiers combined.
waterfall.gpTotalTooltip: Total money the GP receives from all tiers combined.
waterfall.lpMultipleTooltip: LP total received divided by LP equity invested. 2.0x means the LP doubled their money.
waterfall.gpMultipleTooltip: GP total received divided by GP equity invested. Often higher due to promote/carry structure.
waterfall.lpIrrTooltip: LP Internal Rate of Return — the annualized percentage return accounting for project duration.
waterfall.gpIrrTooltip: GP Internal Rate of Return — often higher than LP due to promoted interest in upper tiers.
waterfall.remainderTooltip: Money left over after all tiers are exhausted. Usually means the waterfall tiers don't cover all proceeds.

maturity.title: Debt Maturity Wall
maturity.emptyState: No debt entries. Click "Add Loan" to model your debt maturity wall.

variance.title: Budget vs Actual
variance.selectProjectPrompt: Select a project to enter budget vs actual data.

errors.noProject: Unit is not assigned to a project
